questions.q: What single quantity was Millikan's oil-drop experiment designed to measure directly?
options: The mass of the electron
The charge on a single electron (the elementary charge)
The charge-to-mass ratio of the electron
The viscosity of air
questions.explanation: Millikan set out to measure the fundamental charge \(e\), the charge on one electron. J.J. Thomson had already found the charge-to-mass ratio, but that is two unknowns tangled together; measuring the charge directly was needed to untangle them. The electron's mass came out only as a consequence once \(e\) was known.

questions.q: Why could Thomson's charge-to-mass ratio alone not give the charge of the electron?
options: Thomson's apparatus was too crude to give any reliable numbers
A ratio combines two unknowns, so knowing charge divided by mass does not fix either value on its own
Thomson measured the mass directly but not the charge
The ratio applies only to protons, not electrons
questions.explanation: Thomson measured charge divided by mass — a single ratio holding two unknowns. Just as knowing one number divided by another equals ten does not tell you either number, the ratio could not give the charge by itself. Measuring \(e\) directly was the way to unlock both.

questions.q: Why did Millikan switch from water droplets to oil droplets?
options: Oil carries far more charge than water
Oil droplets are larger and easier to see
Water evaporates quickly, while oil evaporates so slowly the droplet can be observed for a long time
Oil is denser, so the droplets fall faster
questions.explanation: Tiny water droplets evaporated within seconds, shrinking and vanishing before a measurement could be made. The graduate student Harvey Fletcher suggested clock oil, which evaporates so slowly that a droplet can be watched for hours, making the measurement possible.

questions.q: With the electric field switched OFF, the droplet falls at a constant terminal velocity rather than accelerating. Why?
options: Gravity does not act on such a small droplet
The upward air-resistance (drag) force quickly grows to balance the droplet's weight, giving zero net force
The plates repel the droplet upward at constant speed
The droplet is too light for gravity to overcome friction at all
questions.explanation: As the droplet speeds up, the upward Stokes drag increases until it exactly equals the weight. With no net force, the droplet falls at a steady terminal velocity. Timing this fall lets Millikan use Stokes' law to find the radius, and hence the mass, of the droplet.

questions.q: When a charged droplet is held perfectly stationary between the plates, which equation expresses the force balance used to find its charge \(q\)?
options: \( qE = mg \)
\( q = mgE \)
\( qV = md \)
\( mg = 6\pi \eta r v \)
questions.explanation: At balance the upward electric force \(qE\) equals the downward weight \(mg\), so \(qE = mg\). Rearranging with \(E = V/d\) gives \(q = mgd/V\). The Stokes-law expression (option D) governs the field-OFF stage that gives the mass, not the charge balance.

questions.q: A drop of mass \(6.8\times10^{-15}\ \mathrm{kg}\) is held stationary between plates 16 mm apart with 5000 V across them (\(g = 9.8\ \mathrm{m/s^2}\)). Approximately what charge does it carry?
options: \( 3.1\times10^{5}\ \mathrm{C} \)
questions.explanation: The field is \(E = V/d = 5000/0.016 = 3.125\times10^{5}\ \mathrm{V/m}\). Then \(q = mg/E = (6.8\times10^{-15}\times9.8)/(3.125\times10^{5}) \approx 2.1\times10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}\) — about one excess electron's worth within experimental error.

questions.q: Millikan found that every droplet's charge was a whole-number multiple of one tiny value. What does this demonstrate about electric charge?
options: Charge can take any continuous value
Charge is quantised: it comes only in whole-number multiples of the elementary charge \(e\)
Larger droplets always carry larger fundamental charges
Charge depends on the voltage applied to the plates
questions.explanation: Measured charges were always \(q = ne\) for whole numbers \(n\) — never a fraction such as 2.5 or 7.3. This shows charge is quantised, existing only in indivisible lumps of the elementary charge \(e\). It is the direct evidence that charge, like energy in the quantum revolution, comes in countable packets.

questions.q: What was the purpose of firing X-rays into the chamber during the experiment?
options: To heat the oil so it would not evaporate
To make the droplets glow so they could be seen
To ionise the air so a single droplet could gain or lose electrons, changing its charge by whole multiples of \(e\) while being watched
To increase the voltage across the plates automatically
questions.explanation: The X-rays ionised the gas, letting a single droplet pick up or lose electrons mid-experiment. Every time its charge changed, it changed by a clean whole-number multiple of \(e\) — decisive evidence that charge changes one electron at a time and is quantised.

questions.q: Millikan's 1913 value was \(1.592\times10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}\), about 0.6% below the modern value. What was the main source of this small error?
options: He timed the droplets incorrectly
He used a slightly inaccurate published value for the viscosity of air, which fed into his radius calculation
He mistakenly counted droplets with fractional charges
His plate separation was measured wrongly
questions.explanation: Almost the entire discrepancy traces to the air-viscosity value Millikan took from the literature, which was slightly wrong. Because viscosity enters Stokes' law for the droplet radius, the small error propagated into the final charge. His method and reasoning were sound.

questions.q: Modern physics has quarks with charges of \(\pm\tfrac{1}{3}\) and \(\pm\tfrac{2}{3}\) of \(e\). Why does this NOT contradict Millikan's conclusion that free charge is quantised in units of \(e\)?
options: Quarks have since been shown not to exist
Quarks are confined inside particles and can never be isolated, and the charges of any free particle always add to a whole multiple of \(e\)
Quark charges are too small for Millikan's apparatus to detect
Quarks were exactly the subelectrons Ehrenhaft had measured
questions.explanation: Quarks are permanently confined inside protons, neutrons and other particles and cannot be pulled out alone. The charge of any particle you can actually isolate is always a whole-number multiple of \(e\) — for example a proton's quarks (\(+\tfrac{2}{3},+\tfrac{2}{3},-\tfrac{1}{3}\)) sum to \(+e\). Millikan's law holds for every free charge.
